proofs.proof_tex:
The answer is yes. We prove the following finite exact atlas theorem directly from def:generic-full-fiber-compatibility, def:fiber-correspondences, def:real-feasible-regions, and the construction in def:real-atlas-handle. Fix m\ge 1, put d=m+1, K=2d, n=d+1=m+2, q=q_K, and p=p_K. There is an effective finite partition \{B_i\}_{i=1}^N of  ar E_m(\mathbb R) into semialgebraic cells, bits \varepsilon_i^b\in\{0,1\} for b\in\{\mathrm{right},\mathrm{left}\}, and finite cylindrical cell families \{C^b_{i,\alpha}\}_\alpha such that, for every t\in B_i,

\[\varepsilon_i^b=1\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad R^b_{m,K}(t)\cap F^b_{m,K}
e\varnothing,	ag{1}\]

\[R^b_{m,K}(t)\cap F^b_{m,K}= igcup_\alpha\{\lambda:(t,\lambda)\in C^b_{i,\alpha}\}.	ag{2}\]

Every cell in (2) is described recursively by polynomial sign conditions and either an equation to a continuous real-algebraic root function or an open interval between two ordered such root functions. Consequently t has independent centered real non-Gaussian finite-K source representations through order K on both arrows exactly when \varepsilon_i^{\mathrm{right}}=\varepsilon_i^{\mathrm{left}}=1.

Step 1 (an exact finite certificate for one source). For a real cumulant list k=(k_2,\ldots,k_K), set k_1=0 and define

\[\mu_0(k)=1,\qquad \mu_r(k)=B_r(0,k_2,\ldots,k_r)=\sum_{\pi\in\mathcal P([r])}\prod_{A\in\pi}k_{|A|},\quad 1\le r\le K.	ag{3}\]

Thus every \mu_r is an explicit integer-coefficient polynomial in k_2,\ldots,k_r. Let Q_K(k) be the formula asserting that there exist w_h\ge0 and z_h\in\mathbb R, 1\le h\le n, such that

\[\sum_{h=1}^n w_h=1,\qquad \sum_{h=1}^n w_hz_h^r=\mu_r(k)\ (1\le r\le K),\qquad \mu_2(k)>0.	ag{4}\]

We show that Q_K(k) is equivalent to the existence of a centered non-Gaussian real random variable with finite K-th absolute moment and cumulants k_2,\ldots,k_K. If (4) holds, the atomic probability measure 
u=\sum_h w_h\delta_{z_h} has the moments (3), is centered, and has positive variance. It has finite moments of every order. A finite atomic law of positive variance is not Gaussian, and the triangular moment-cumulant identities recover its cumulants as k_2,\ldots,k_K.

Conversely, let 
u be any source law required by def:real-feasible-regions. Its variance is positive, since variance zero would make it the degenerate Gaussian \delta_0. Write its moments as \mu_0,\ldots,\mu_{2d} and form the positive-semidefinite Hankel matrix H_d=(\mu_{a+b})_{0\le a,b\le d}. If H_d is singular, some nonzero polynomial h of degree at most d satisfies

\[0=\operatorname{coef}(h)^	op H_d\operatorname{coef}(h)=\int h(x)^2\,d
u(x).	ag{5}\]

Hence 
u is supported on the real zero set of h, which has at most d points. Appending zero-weight atoms gives (4) with n=d+1 atoms.

If H_d is positive definite, choose any real extension moment \mu_{2d+1}. There is a unique monic polynomial g(x)=x^{d+1}+\sum_{a=0}^d g_ax^a satisfying L(gx^k)=0 for 0\le k\le d, where L(x^r)=\mu_r for 0\le r\le2d+1: its coefficient matrix is H_d. Give the polynomials of degree at most d the inner product \langle f,hangle=L(fh), and let Tf be the remainder of xf modulo g. Since g is orthogonal to every polynomial of degree at most d,

\[\langle Tf,hangle=L(xfh)=\langle f,Thangle.	ag{6}\]

Thus T is a real symmetric operator. A finite-dimensional real symmetric operator has an orthonormal eigenbasis: a maximizer of its Rayleigh quotient is an eigenvector, and induction on the invariant orthogonal complement completes the diagonalization. Let (z_h,e_h), 1\le h\le n, be its eigenpairs and put w_h=\langle1,e_hangle^2. The vector 1 is cyclic because T^a1=x^a for 0\le a\le d. Therefore every eigenspace is one-dimensional and no e_h is orthogonal to 1; otherwise a nonzero vector in that eigenspace would be orthogonal to every T^a1. Hence w_h>0 and \sum_hw_h=\langle1,1angle=1. For every 0\le r\le2d, choose a,b\le d with a+b=r. The spectral expansion and (6) give

\[\sum_{h=1}^n w_hz_h^r=\langle T^r1,1angle=\langle T^a1,T^b1angle=L(x^{a+b})=\mu_r.	ag{7}\]

This proves (4), and therefore proves the exact equivalence of Q_K with finite-K source feasibility, including singular moment sequences.

For a right parameter \lambda=(\gamma,ho,c), let \operatorname{Feas}^{\mathrm{right}}(\lambda) be the finite polynomial formula consisting of \gamma
e0, all pairwise inequalities among \gamma,ho_1,\ldots,ho_m, and Q_K(c_j) for all 0\le j\le m+1. Define \operatorname{Feas}^{\mathrm{left}} analogously. The preceding equivalence and the product construction for finitely many marginal atomic laws imply

\[\operatorname{Feas}^b(\lambda)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad\lambda\in F^b_{m,K}.	ag{8}\]

The product construction supplies mutual independence, so (8) omits no cross-source condition.

Step 2 (equations for the complex exceptional closure). Let \Delta_{\mathrm{right}}(	heta) and \Delta_{\mathrm{left}}(\eta) be the respective nonvanishing products defining the two generic parameter loci. Over \mathbb C, introduce an auxiliary variable s and form

\[I_{\mathrm{right}}=\langle\Phi^{\mathrm{right}}(	heta)-t,\ \Phi^{\mathrm{left}}(\eta)-t,\ s\Delta_{\mathrm{right}}(	heta)-1angle,	ag{9}\]

with I_{\mathrm{left}} defined by replacing the last equation with s\Delta_{\mathrm{left}}(\eta)-1. The equation s\Delta_b-1 is solvable exactly on the b-generic locus. Use multivariate polynomial division with an elimination order and put

\[J_{\mathrm{right}}=I_{\mathrm{right}}\cap\mathbb C[t],\qquad J_{\mathrm{left}}=I_{\mathrm{left}}\cap\mathbb C[t],\qquad J_m=J_{\mathrm{right}}\cap J_{\mathrm{left}}.	ag{10}\]

For completeness, the elimination assertion used here follows algebraically as follows. Every polynomial in an elimination ideal vanishes on the corresponding incidence projection and hence on its Zariski closure. In the reverse direction, division by an elimination Groebner basis eliminates every leading monomial involving a parameter variable; a polynomial consequence depending only on t therefore has zero remainder precisely when it belongs to the t-part of the basis. The common zero set of that t-part is the smallest algebraic set containing the projection. Termination follows because each nonzero remainder strictly enlarges the generated leading-monomial ideal, while an increasing chain of monomial ideals in finitely many variables stabilizes (induct on the number of variables and the least exponent of the last variable). Thus finite generators of (10) are computable. Since a union of algebraic sets is cut out by the intersection of their ideals, def:generic-full-fiber-compatibility gives

\[ ar E_m(\mathbb R)=\{t\in\mathbb R^q:f(t)=0	ext{ for every }f\in J_m\}.	ag{11}\]

The maps have real coefficients, so real and imaginary parts of a finite generating set give a finite real polynomial list for (11). This includes real points lying only in the complex Zariski closure, not merely points already in E_m.

Step 3 (the complete feasible incidence sets). For b\in\{\mathrm{right},\mathrm{left}\}, define

\[\Gamma_b=\{(t,\lambda)\in\mathbb R^q	imes\mathbb R^p:J_m(t)=0,\ \Phi^b_{m,K}(\lambda)=t,\ \operatorname{Feas}^b(\lambda)\}.	ag{12}\]

Here each occurrence of Q_K is the existential polynomial formula (4). By def:fiber-correspondences and (8),

\[(\Gamma_b)_t=R^b_{m,K}(t)\cap F^b_{m,K}.	ag{13}\]

No genericity, Jacobian-rank restriction, or quotient by source relabelings has been imposed in (12); hence (13) is the full feasible fiber, including every singular and nongeneric branch allowed by F^b_{m,K}.

Step 4 (simultaneous cylindrical construction). Collect the finitely many polynomials in (4), (11), and (12), with coordinate order t first, then \lambda, then all atomic witnesses (w,z). Construct a sign-invariant cylindrical decomposition recursively. In one variable, isolate and order all real roots and use the roots and complementary intervals as cells. Before lifting in a new variable x, adjoin the coefficients in x, the discriminants, and all principal subresultant coefficients of each pair of current polynomials and of each polynomial with its derivative. On a cell where these projection polynomials have fixed signs, degrees and root multiplicities cannot change, and two ordered real roots cannot meet or cross without a relevant discriminant or subresultant vanishing. The real roots consequently form finitely many ordered continuous semialgebraic section functions; their graphs and the open sectors between consecutive graphs are the lifted cells. Induction yields finitely many cells on which every input polynomial has constant sign. Cells lying on boundaries may be recursively refined by the same projection polynomials, yielding a finite frontier-compatible semialgebraic cell partition if `stratification' is required to include the frontier condition.

Now eliminate the atomic witnesses cellwise. On each (t,\lambda)-cell, Q_K is true exactly when at least one witness cell in its cylindrical stack satisfies all equations and weak/strict inequalities in (4). Retain precisely those (t,\lambda)-cells. Their union is \Gamma_b. At the t level retain the cells contained in (11); call them B_i. Cylindricity implies that each retained (t,\lambda)-cell projects onto one entire B_i and is recursively a section or sector over it. Denote these cells by C^b_{i,\alpha}. Equation (13) now proves (2). Moreover, a selected \Gamma_b-stack exists over one point of B_i if and only if it exists over every point of B_i. Defining \varepsilon_i^b to be its nonemptiness proves (1). Applying (1) to both arrows gives the asserted exact both-arrow label.

Every boundary case is retained and decided by a sign cell: zero atomic weights, repeated atomic support points, singular Hankel matrices, unbounded parameter sectors, and nongeneric opposite-arrow fibers are not discarded. Loading collisions and a zero direct edge are excluded exactly when def:real-feasible-regions excludes them.

Finally, the construction is a finite algorithm for every fixed m. Prior to saturation elimination, a simultaneous two-arrow run has q+2p+4n^2 real coordinates: q observable coordinates, p structural coordinates per arrow, and 2n^2 atomic-witness coordinates per arrow. The cumulant and moment equations have degree at most K+1, while the saturation products have explicitly computable degree 1+m+m(m-1)/2+n(K-1). If s_m and D_m are the computable number and maximum degree of the polynomial list after the finite elimination in (10), the projection-and-lifting recursion takes at most (s_mD_m)^{2^{O(q+2p+4n^2)}} arithmetic and sign operations, the standard doubly-exponential scale visible directly from the repeated coefficient/subresultant construction. Thus the output is an evaluable exact atlas, not an unspecified existential characterization. This proves the open-ended statement.
